First, introduce the research background of this paper.Copper is widely used in heat-transfer units such as evaporators, air-conditioners and refrigerators due to the unique combination of relative strength, corrosion resistance and higher thermal conductivity. As for current situation, despite a lot of premature failure had been prevented effectively, a large amount of failures still exist.Formicary corrosion is also called ant nest corrosion. The term comes from the similarity of the morphology to an ant’s nest.
将上面的一段文本改为口语化的英语演讲稿

Good evening, everyone. Today I'd like to talk about the research background for our paper. 

Copper is a valuable material for heat-transfer units like air-conditioners, refrigerators, and evaporators. This is because of its super strength, excellent ability to resist corrosion, and its high thermal conductivity. 

However, there is a catch. Despite our best efforts, there are still a considerable amount of failures happening with copper applications in these units. One such type of corrosion that causes premature failure is known as formicary corrosion, or as some call it, "ant nest corrosion." 

This strange name comes from the similar appearance of the corrosion's morphology to that of a little ant nest. It's a big problem, and we want to find a way to stop it. So, that's what we're researching, and we wanted to share it with you. Thanks for your attention.

Let me tell you about my experimental method. Copper tube specimens of 40 mm in length, suspended at 45° in glass test tube, were exposed to the concentration of 1 vol% formic acid vapor in airtight glass vessels for different days. At the same time, thermal cycling experiments were conducted, holding the temperature by immersing the airtight glass vessels in a thermostatically controlled water bath at 40 °C for 16 h followed by 8 h at 25 °C to cover the first 24 h cycle. Thermal cycling was continued for up to 30 days. 
将上面一段文本改为口语化的英语演讲稿

Okay, so let me tell you about the method I used for my experiment. 

I took copper tube samples that were 40mm in length and hung them at 45 degrees inside glass test tubes. Then, I exposed them to a concentration of 1% formic acid vapor in tightly sealed glass containers for various amounts of time. 

At the same time, I did thermal cycling experiments. This means I held the temperature by putting the glass containers in a water bath set at a controlled temperature of 40 degrees Celsius for 16 hours, then changed it to 25 degrees Celsius for 8 hours to make up the 24-hour cycle. I repeated these cycles for up to 30 days. 

It might sound complicated, but it's a great way to test the effects of formic acid on copper tubes. I hope this information helps you understand my research a bit better. Thank you for listening.

At the same time, I did thermal cycling experiments. This means I held the temperature by putting the glass containers in a water bath set at a controlled temperature of 40 degrees Celsius for 16 hours, then changed it to 25 degrees Celsius for 8 hours to make up the 24-hour cycle. I repeated these cycles for up to 30 days.

So, I also conducted thermal cycling experiments alongside the formic acid exposure. What that means is that I controlled the temperature by placing the sealed glass containers in a water bath that was set at 40 degrees Celsius for 16 hours, and then I switched the temperature to 25 degrees Celsius for 8 hours to complete the 24-hour cycle. 

And the best part is, I repeated these cycles for up to 30 days, which allowed us to get a better understanding of the long-term effects of formic acid on copper tubes. It was a very thorough experiment, and I'm excited to share my findings with you all. Thank you for listening.

At the same time, I did thermal cycling experiments. This means I held the temperature by putting the glass containers in a water bath set at a controlled temperature of 40 degrees Celsius for 16 hours, then changed it to 25 degrees Celsius for 8 hours to make up the 24-hour cycle. I repeated these cycles for up to 30 days.将上段文本改为被动态的口语演讲稿

Thermal cycling experiments were also conducted during the formic acid exposure. In this process, the glass containers were placed in a water bath with a controlled temperature of 40°C for 16 hours. After that, the temperature was changed to 25°C for 8 hours, which completed the 24-hour cycle. This process was then repeated for up to 30 days, allowing us to observe the long-term effects of formic acid on copper tubes. Thank you for listening.

We can see the difference from the comparison of the metallographic diagram of the simulated experimental copper pipe and the copper pipe under real working conditions. The morphology of ant nest corrosion has many branches, and at the intersection of these branches was the starting point where corrosion occurs.  The corrosion tunnels in simulation experiment are obviously wider than leakage tube. 将上段文本改为口语演讲稿

Let me show you the difference we observed when comparing the simulated experimental copper pipe to a copper pipe used in actual working conditions. By examining the metallographic diagram, we can see that the morphology of ant nest corrosion has many branches, and at the intersections of these branches, corrosion begins to occur. 

Interestingly, we found that the corrosion tunnels in our simulation experiments were wider than the leakage tubes we observed in real working pipes. This information is vital in helping us better understand the nature of ant nest corrosion, and we hope it will lead to better solutions in tackling this issue. Thank you for listening.
The difference we observed when comparing the simulated experimental copper pipe to a copper pipe used in actual working conditions.将这段文本口语化

So, when we compared the experimental copper pipe simulation to an actual copper pipe used in real working conditions, we could see some significant differences. The morphology of ant nest corrosion had many branches, and corrosion began to occur at the intersections of these branches. 

Interestingly, we found that the corrosion tunnels in our simulated experiments were wider than the leakage tubes we observed in real-world copper pipes. This new insight is crucial in helping us better understand ant nest corrosion, and we hope it will lead us towards finding better solutions to prevent it from happening. Thank you for listening.

The surface morphology of leakage copper tubes observed by SEM. Several micro-cracks can be seen obliviously. Actually, these micro-cracks are the laminations/folds which could have been produced during tube manufacturing. The pitting holes are distributed regularly, almost all pitting distribute on the bottom of internal groove.
将这段文本口语化

We observed the surface morphology of the copper tubes that had experienced leakage, using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). What we found was that several micro-cracks were visible on the surface, but in reality, these were laminations or folds that may have occurred during the tube manufacturing process. 

We also noticed that the pitting holes were distributed regularly and were mostly located on the bottom of the internal groove. This information tells us a lot about the nature of the corrosion and will help us in designing better manufacturing processes for copper tubes. Thanks for listening.

The metallographic morphology of copper tubes. (a, b is the sample which was used in formic simulation experiment. c, d is the failure copper tubes which was used in practical application.)  The edges of the ant nest corrosion tunnels propagate along grain boundaries and the edges of the stress corrosion tunnels exhibit transgranular fracture. The stress corrosion cracking process normally propagates by intergranular fashion with multiple branching.

Let me show you some images of the metallographic morphology of copper tubes. The samples marked (a, b) were used in our formic acid simulation experiment, while samples (c, d) were taken from copper tubes that had failed in practical applications.

Upon close inspection, we noticed that the edges of the ant nest corrosion tunnels propagated along grain boundaries. On the other hand, the edges of the stress corrosion tunnels exhibited transgranular fracture. 

Additionally, we found that the process of stress corrosion cracking typically propagated through an intergranular fashion with multiple branching. These details provide invaluable insights into the nature of ant nest corrosion, and we hope to leverage them to find more effective ways to prevent it from occurring. Thank you for listening.

Let me show you some images of the metallographic morphology of copper tubes. The samples marked (a, b) were used in our formic acid simulation experiment, while samples (c, d) were taken from copper tubes that had failed in practical applications.

I'd like to share with you some images of the metallographic morphology of copper tubes. The samples labeled (a, b) were used during our formic acid simulation experiment, while samples (c, d) were taken from copper tubes that had failed in practical applications.

As we examined these images, we noticed that the edges of the ant nest corrosion tunnels were propagating along grain boundaries, while the edges of the stress corrosion tunnels exhibited a transgranular fracture. 

Interestingly, we also observed that stress corrosion cracking typically propagated through an intergranular fashion with multiple branching. These findings provide us with incredibly useful insights into the nature of ant nest corrosion and will help us develop more effective strategies for preventing it in the future. Thank you for listening.
得到以下结论

So, in conclusion, our research has helped us to better understand the nature of ant nest corrosion in copper tubes. We found that the corrosion tunnels tend to propagate along grain boundaries in the case of ant nest corrosion, while the edges of stress corrosion tunnels exhibit transgranular fracture. 

We also observed that formic acid can cause micro-cracks in copper tubes, and stress corrosion cracking can propagate through an intergranular fashion, with multiple branching. 

These new insights have significant implications for copper tube manufacturing processes and can help us develop better preventative strategies to avoid ant nest corrosion in the future. Thank you for listening.

我们可以得到以下结论The interaction of ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion that lead to the failure of copper tubes. The stress corrosion accelerates the corrosion rates of ant nest corrosion.

Owing to stress corrosion accelerate the corrosion rates, the corrosion products don't have enough time to accumulate and expand leading to the morphology of interactive corrosion is thinner than single ant nest corrosion.

The corrosion starting point also exist difference. The single ant nest corrosion starting point is irregular. In contrast, starting point of the interactive corrosion tends to distribute at the bottom of internal groove where the tensile stress is concentrated.

Based on our research findings, we can draw the conclusion that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion accelerates the overall corrosion rate of copper tubes, which ultimately leads to their failure. 

Interestingly, because stress corrosion accelerates corrosion rates, corrosion products don't accumulate enough to produce an expansion effect, resulting in an interactive corrosion morphology that is thinner than that of single ant nest corrosion. 

Moreover, the starting points for single ant nest corrosion is often irregular, while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest. 

Overall, these conclusions help us to better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can lead to more effective preventative measures in future applications. Thank you for listening.

Good morning, everyone. My name is <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>. Next, I will show my paper through the following PowerPoint.
The title of my paper is the Interactive effect of ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion on the failure of copper tubes. 

2
I’d like to talk about the research background for our paper.
Copper is a valuable material for heat-transfer units like air-conditioners, refrigerators, and evaporators. This is because of its super strength, excellent ability to resist corrosion, and its high thermal conductivity.
However, there is a problem. Despite our best efforts, there are still a considerable amount of failures happening with copper applications in these units. One such type of corrosion that causes premature failure is known as formicary corrosion, or as some call it, “ant nest corrosion.”
This name comes from the similar appearance of the corrosion’s morphology to that of a little ant nest. It’s a big problem, and we want to find out how it happen. 

3
I took copper tube samples that were 40mm in length and hung them at 45 degrees inside glass test tubes. Then, I exposed them to a concentration of 1% formic acid vapor in tightly sealed glass containers for various amounts of time.
Thermal cycling experiments were also conducted during the formic acid exposure. In this process, the glass containers were placed in a water bath with a controlled temperature of 40°C for 16 hours. After that, the temperature was changed to 25°C for 8 hours, which completed the 24-hour cycle. This process was then repeated for up to 30 days.

4
We observed the surface morphology of the copper tubes that had experienced leakage, using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). What we found was that several micro-cracks were visible on the surface, but in reality, these were laminations or folds that may have occurred during the tube manufacturing process.
We also noticed that the pitting holes were distributed regularly and were mostly located on the bottom of the internal groove.

5
When we compared the experimental copper pipe to an actual copper pipe used in real working conditions, we could see some differences.
By examining the morphology photos, we can see that the morphology of ant nest corrosion has many branches, and at the intersections of these branches, corrosion begins to occur.
Interestingly, we found that the corrosion tunnels in our simulation experiments were wider than the leakage tubes we observed in real working pipes.


6
These are some images of the metallographic morphology of copper tubes. 
We noticed that the edges of the ant nest corrosion tunnels propagated along grain boundaries. On the other hand, the edges of the stress corrosion tunnels exhibited transgranular fracture.
Additionally, we found that the process of stress corrosion cracking typically propagated through an intergranular fashion with multiple branching.

7
Based on our research findings, we can draw the conclusion that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion accelerates the overall corrosion rate of copper tubes, which ultimately leads to their failure.
Interestingly, because stress corrosion accelerates corrosion rates, corrosion products don’t accumulate enough to produce an expansion effect, resulting in an interactive corrosion morphology that is thinner than that of single ant nest corrosion.
Moreover, the starting points for single ant nest corrosion is often irregular, while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest.
Overall, these conclusions help us to better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can lead to more effective preventative measures in future applications. Thank you for liste
将上述文本简化到500 words. 不改变文本大意

Good day everyone, my name is <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON> and the title of my paper is "Interactive effect of ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion on the failure of copper tubes". Copper is an excellent material for heat transfer units such as air conditioners and refrigerators due to its high thermal conductivity and corrosion resistance. However, ant nest corrosion remains a big issue leading to premature failures of copper tubes.

In this study, I exposed copper tubes to 1% formic acid vapor and conducted thermal cycling experiments for up to 30 days. I also observed the surface morphology of the copper tubes using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and compared the experimental copper tubes to actual copper tubes used in real working conditions.

Results showed that the ant nest corrosion tunnels propagated along grain boundaries while the stress corrosion tunnels exhibited transgranular fracture. Stress corrosion accelerated corrosion rates, leading to thinner corrosion tunnels compared to ant nest corrosion alone. Also, the starting points for ant nest corrosion are often irregular while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest.

In conclusion, our findings suggest that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion is responsible for the premature failure of copper tubes. These results provide valuable insights into the mechanisms behind copper tube failures caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can aid the development of effective preventative measures for better future applications. Thank you.

Based on our research findings, we can draw the conclusion that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion accelerates the overall corrosion rate of copper tubes, which ultimately leads to their failure.
Interestingly, because stress corrosion accelerates corrosion rates, corrosion products don’t accumulate enough to produce an expansion effect, resulting in an interactive corrosion morphology that is thinner than that of single ant nest corrosion.
Moreover, the starting points for single ant nest corrosion is often irregular, while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest.
Overall, these conclusions help us to better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can lead to more effective preventative measures in future applications. Thank you for listening.

Our research findings conclude that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion accelerates the corrosion rate of copper tubes, leading to their failure. We also found that stress corrosion leads to thinner corrosion tunnels, and interactive corrosion starting points concentrate at the bottom of the internal groove where tensile stress is highest. These conclusions help us better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure and can lead to more effective preventative measures for future applications.

Interestingly, because stress corrosion accelerates corrosion rates, corrosion products don’t accumulate enough to produce an expansion effect, resulting in an interactive corrosion morphology that is thinner than that of single ant nest corrosion.
Moreover, the starting points for single ant nest corrosion is often irregular, while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest.
Overall, these conclusions help us to better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can lead to more effective preventative measures in future applications. Thank you for listening.

The stress corrosion also results in thinner corrosion tunnels and the starting points for interactive corrosion concentrate at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest. Our research provides valuable insights into the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can aid in developing more effective preventative measures for future applications. Thank you.
